sentences.text: No, not with my children.
With my husband.
Yes.
Yeah.
R-E-N.
Yeah.
Laren is 27 kilometers southeast from Amsterdam.
Very close to get to Amsterdam.
Yes.
And it was an-- it is an artist colony.
Like Woodstock here in America.
In Laren.
Yeah.
I didn't-- we didn't select them.
They-- the-- the organization, the underground organization, of-- we were members, of course-- would tell of people who were in trouble and who could take them.
No.
We called it just the group.
The group.
And did it have any affiliation with any religious group?
No.
No.
No.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
We were very careful.
Well, they were citizens of the village.
They were all from Laren.
Yeah.
They were all-- well, also a few from the little villages around.
Well, [INAUDIBLE], for instance.
And Naaiden.
N-A-A-I-D-E-N.
Naaiden.
And Huyzen-- H-U-Y-Z-E-N.
These were all people who lived--
They're all little villages around there.
That I don't remember.
You mean in the underground group.
No.
That I don't remember.
Oh, yes.
We had meetings together.
Sometimes in my--
Sometimes-- oh, very secretive.
And sometimes in our house, and sometimes in one of the other houses.
But we tried to avoid meetings as much possible.
So we-- we usually related with messenger girls, we called them.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Couriers--
--we called them.
Well, they were--
They were either young girls or young women who had affiliation with our group--
--their parents, or whatever.
And they brought messages.
Well, they would go on the bicycle and always knew exactly where to go.
But they had a pass.
And the pass was a-- a name written on a piece of paper and then put in two pieces-- how you say-- ripped it.
Ripped it.
And then she kept one of them.
And through the organization, the person where she was [INAUDIBLE] got the other part.
So when she came to a house of people she didn't know, she had her paper, and they fit it-- where it fitted together.
And then they knew they could trust each other.
For what reason?
Well, when we had to bring-- oh, when they were caught?
Well, when they were stopped, there was a good chance they were taken, of course, if they had not of-- a good story-- of a good--
Well, that they-- that they didn't know anything about it, that they were going to a girlfriend, or whatever, you know.
Most of those things happened in a-- on bicycles.
Because, first of all, in Holland, everybody goes on a bicycle.
And secondly, we had no cars anymore.
So-- but you were not too often stopped on the road.

But you could fall in a trap, for instance, when-- when a house was invaded, and the Jews were taken, who were in the house-- they had found Jewish people there and they would-- had taken them-- then they also took the-- the people who lived in that house.
And then they left, usually, one of their soldiers, or men, in that empty house.
And then when-- everybody who came to that house was taken.
So you were always very careful.
For instance, we had a geranium.
And the geranium was standing in one window, always.
When there was danger, that-- that-- the moment I saw German soldiers around the house, or in-- in the road, I took the geranium and put it in another window.
That meant for all our members, don't come to the house.
And so when we were taken, we were absolutely sure that that thing was there.
That the geranium was not--
That was a-- that was a very good thing.
Were taken?
Well, they came, for instance, quite often, to our house, and interrogated us.
And that was very suspenseful and very nerve racking, [LAUGHS] but--
Oh, the couriers.
Yeah, once in awhile--
Yeah--
They got through.
Yeah.
In general.
Very once in awhile, one was caught.
Well, strategies.
We had to, you know, discuss, and, you know, and--
Well, about everything, practically, which we had to do.
Yeah.

Getting-- for instance, when we knew of people who lived in another part of Holland, and how we could get to them, and how we could get in touch with to help them and get them out, and things like that.
All about the needs for the Jewish people.
That's what it was all about.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, well, we just told them we have a meeting tonight.
I don't know.
It was up to everybody--
--self.
Yeah.
Well, we-- you couldn't disguise that.
There was no way of disguising it-- we were a group of Jewish people together.
There was no way to disguise that.
Oh, there were no Jews.
Yeah, there were a few-- yeah that's true.
I remember.
Eddie.
He was a Jewish man.
Yeah, there were Jewish people in, too, yeah.
Some of the Jewish people who were not hidden and were not taken yet.
Yes.
That was terrible, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, that-- well, you try to-- first of all, I tried always to be very amazed.
Why do you come to my house?
I have nothing to do with all this.
You know, that was your attitude.
And then he came, and he asked tricky questions and so on, but--
Have you Jewish friends?
Do you know any Jews?
Do you know where they are?
You know, things like that.
Or have you ever been affiliated with any work for Jewish people, or whatever.
[DRINKS]
Oh, yes.
We have had narrow escapes, yeah.
Very narrow.
Well, for instance, once they came, and we were absolutely not aware of it that they were coming.
Usually-- you see, we lived a little bit on the outside of a village-- Laren was a village-- and there was one policeman who was in our group.
Nobody else knew that, of course, than our group members.
And he had-- had taken care of the fact that we had a telephone-- we were-- that our telephone was connected.
Because we lived on a dead end road which ended-- ended in very large nature reservation, with [INAUDIBLE].
And he played up very much to the German commander, the danger of a fire in the [INAUDIBLE].
Because the Germans would also have maneuvers there in the-- in the-- in that place.
And so they said, we need somebody who lives there, who we can trust, and who must have a telephone to warn when there is a fire.
And under that pretext, we had a telephone.
Of course, it didn't help us much, because no-- none of our friends or anybody else had a telephone.
But it helped us only, very much, with this policeman.
Because when the Germans came to our village, where we lived a little bit outside, he would give us a call.
That means he would ring twice and hang up.
Then we knew there was danger.
Then all of the Jewish people could hide.
And that helped us tremendously, of course.
Yeah.
I had several, you know.
Oh, yeah.
That one time-- that was the worst time.
We had-- we had an-- we called it-- how we called it again?
I don't remember.
We had a funny word for it.
It wouldn't come.
Anyway, that was a package where all secret papers were in.
For instance, passports of Jewish people and the names where they were hidden, et cetera-- so that was deadly dangerous, that package.
And for that reason, we moved it very often, from one house to the other.
And one day, my husband came to-- a friend of ours from our organization, Jan Bartelsman, was caught.
And the last thing we knew that he had the package.
But his wife told us that he had brought it the night before-- he was-- he happened to bring it to his mother-in-law.
So we knew the package, as we called it-- which was red hot dangerous-- that it was with his mother.
But when he was caught, there was always a chance that they would go to all his family members.
So my husband raced to, on the bicycle, to his mother, who lived in the same village, and said, I know you have the package.
Give it to me.
We'll hide it.
It's too dangerous to be with you.
So he brought that back and, with a Jewish man who was hidden with us, they dug a hole.
And when-- they didn't tell me anything.
But it was in-- they had wrapped it in tar paper.
And so I saw them digging there in the garden and I thought, well, what are they doing there?
You know.
We were always stressed with fear and all.
So I thought, what are they doing there?
Something I don't know.
So I went there.
And I said what are you doing.
And he said, oh, we-- I'm burying a dead rabbit.
And then I saw the package with the tar paper.
I said that's a very honorable thing, to be buried that way, for a rabbit.
Is that really a rabbit?
And then my husband said, well, as far as you've seen us-- it's a rabbit.
We didn't tell things each other what we did, because the least you know, the better you were off.
So he said, I didn't want you to know, but it is the package.
Because there is danger in the village today.
So we had that package.
And there were also stamps from the German leader we had stolen.
Not we, ourselves, but in our group, they had stolen stamps, which we used to falsify papers.
And then there was a raid.
And they came to this friend who had had that package and used the stamps.
And they-- they took him, of course.
And they said, we know that you have those things.
And he said, well, I don't have those.
I've never seen them.
You know, he denied it, of course.
And then they-- my husband had gone there just before they came to him, because they had been to somebody-- how did we know-- that I don't remember exactly.
But I know that my husband went just before they came and got the package out of his house and buried it in our garden.
And then when they started to torture him-- well, who was it again?
Well, yeah, when they tortured him, finally he couldn't stand it any longer.
You know, they put weights in his ear lobes, that his ear lobes were hanging till here, you know, and-- This is Jan--
That is Jan Bartelsman.
And then he sent them to his mother-- or-- or his mother-in-law, that I don't remember exactly.
Anyway, we knew that the package was there.
My husband went there because when-- when Jan was taken, we knew that they would go to his family.
And so he went to his mother-in-law and said, give me the package.
I know you have it.
And he brought it to us and we hid it in our garden.
And I didn't know that he had done that, because we didn't tell each other the things we did.
That was much better.
And-- so, but when I saw him digging, then, so, that's how I found out that we had that stuff.
And then when the Germans came and said, you have-- because [?
Joel ?] had said that it was-- or somebody had told that it was with his mother-in-law.
And his mother-in-law said, no, it's not with me.
And when he pressed her, she said, well the last one I know who had it was Aart Vos, which was my husband.
And so they came to our house.
And how did it happen again-- oh, yeah.
We kept denying it.
They brought-- they brought-- I-- no.
They had taken Jan Bartelsman, that was the one with whom we were in this thing.
And Jan Bartelsman came in shackles and his whole face all bruised, and terrible shape.
And he said-- the-- the Germans who brought him to us, shackled-- said that Jan had confessed that we had this package-- under torture, of course.
And we said-- we denied it, of course.
We said-- and my husband wasn't home.
I was alone home with the children.
And I said, no, I don't-- absolutely don't know what package.
What is the package?
You know, I played it totally innocent.
And they said in-- they said we-- we-- we know of a package.
And we know that there are stamps with German signatures from Seyss-Inquart, who was the German leader in Holland.
And so they wanted to have that stamp.
But, of course, when I said we had it, or confessed, then we would have been taken and shot.
And so I kept denying.
But finally, it got known in-- in our village, that the Germans were in our house, with this Jan.
And so his wife heard it.
And his wife couldn't-- couldn't care for the danger for herself.
She wanted to see her husband.
Because she knew she probably would never see him again.
So she came on the bicycle to our house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
How did it go again?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
They asked her for the package.
And-- and then Jan said, I know where it is.
It is with my brother.
And I knew that wasn't true.
I knew where it was.
We-- we had it.
And Jan said that because he knew that they would never be able to find his brother, and that was a way to get those Germans out of the house, at least.
And so I led his wife out to the front door, and I said to her, in 10 minutes on the corner of the lane, be there.
And so I came back and they asked all the time for my husband.
And finally, I said, I think I know where my husband is.
And he might know where that package is.
So if you give me 10 minutes, then I'll go to the neighbors, who have a telephone, and I'll try to find my husband.
Oh, yeah.
My husband had also-- yeah.
My husband had phoned already.
Because he'd-- he heard in the village that there was trouble in our house.
So he phoned.
Yeah.
We had our phone because we were the fire--
--watchers for the [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and he went-- I don't know where he went.
I think to that policeman, who had a phone, and who was good, as we called it.
You were either good or bad in the war.
You were not nice, or whatever.
You were good--
--or you were--
--bad.
They called it you have butter on your head.
That meant that you were in the organization.
For some silly reason, he called it, those people have butter on their head.
Butter-- butter on their head.
I don't know why that's how that--
Yeah.
Yeah.
I don't know where that expression came from.
It just--
Yeah.
Mm-hmm.
So he went to the policeman.
He went to the policeman.
Yeah.
But how did it go with that package?
I don't remember.
But I know it was here at the moment he said, they needed stamps.
And I knew in the package were some stamps.
So when they-- when the Germans were there and the wife of this man who was in shackles came-- young woman, of course, yet-- he said-- Jan, who was taken-- said to her, go to my brother.
Because he might know where that package is.
And so I brought her to the front door.
And I said, in 10 minutes, on the corner here.
And then in 10 minutes, I said-- because he kept asking for my husband-- so after 10 minutes, I said to them, can I go away 10 minutes, then I will try to find my husband.
Because I think I know where he is.
And so they said, all right.
You have 10 minutes.
And I said then, to this girlfriend, 10 minutes on the corner.
And so I went there, and we get-- to get over a small bush-- it's about this high-- from the [INAUDIBLE] to our house.
And we crawled on our belly-- you know, like this-- through those bushes.
Because the man who was interrogating was sitting at the window.
It looked straight to that place.
And Jan, who was, well, allowed to see us, was sitting with his back to it, was just the wrong way around.
So what we had to crawl on our belly and did that.
And then with our hands, we grabbed the thing, and we took those things out.
And I gave them to her, and I said, you must come in 10 or 20 minutes, and act very [PANTING] you know, like you have been or have made a long bicycle trip, and tell that your brother had it.
In the meantime, the brother disappeared, of course.
And so we played it that way, and that worked.
And what happened to the package?
She took the stamps out and gave them to him.
Yes.
Yeah.
At home, with us.
Beg your pardon?
Yeah, but they didn't know what it meant.
We never-- we-- they were very young, yet.
We were young people in that time, you know?
It's long ago.
They were-- we had-- we had an-- a coal bin, with a false bottom.
And you could-- I have a picture of that coal bin.
I-- he-- you could go in there and then there was a long slough.
I've never been in it myself, but my husband has been in it.
Yeah.
A tunnel under the ground.
Well--
I don't know, I would say the length of-- a little longer than this wall.
That's about 12 feet, I would say.
10, 12 feet.
They could crawl in.
Not-- not stand up, of course.
No.
Yeah, it did, that leaded to the [INAUDIBLE] reservation, where it came out, and where they could then--
--if-- if they saw-- looked out of the hole and saw that it was safe, then they could get out there and disappear.
Oh, all the Jewish people, yeah.
I myself with my children.
And we had a studio.
My first husband was an artist.
And we used that studio, also, very much for the hiding people.
And then I always said that it was for my second husband, who was not an artist, but--
Beg your pardon?
An-- an artist's studio.
A painter-- painter's studio.
It was in back of the house.
Very close.
I went there just last summer a-- a few months ago-- weeks ago.
And then I went to see it and then they had added to the house.
They had built-- it was from here till, well, the end of this room somewhere.
Nothing.
But it was a place to hide.
But it-- they could go in there.
And they had-- there was-- there was a hole in a wall.
And it was so meticulously made that it-- it was a wall of from all wood.
And they had slid open one carefully and then you could push that away.
And then you could get under that coal bin, which was outside.
And from the coal bin, in the tunnel.
Yeah.
No, you couldn't get in from the outside, because it had a lid.
You know.
It was a high coal bin.
And it had a lid.
And we-- and we had made a double bottom in it, so that you could get in from the house through a wall, which you'd move.
Well, they could all go in as they did it one after the other, because it would end up in the reservation [INAUDIBLE].
But you could only get into the false wall through the studio.
Only through the coal bin, yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
And we had meeting.
Oh, oh, you mean--
--Jewish people.
No, there-- no.
Well, there came--
Oh, yeah.
Our friends came to our house, and they didn't know what we were doing.
Some did, who [INAUDIBLE] in our own group.
I don't know.
Well, we had razias, of course.
We called it razias.
That was--
--when you got an-- how you say that?
A raid.
Yeah.
A raid is an razia.
Well, they would come to your door, and when you opened, they put your-- their foot in already that you couldn't close it again and they came in and they searched your house.
No.
They've never found one of them.
Two are caught, who tried to escape to Switzerland.
Was a young married couple.
And they couldn't stand being locked up and hidden.
They-- they-- they-- you know, they rebel too much.
And they said, we are going to try to Switzerland.
And they were caught at-- at the frontier and shot, both.
But all the others survived.
Yeah.
Well, one--
No.
No.
Not when they didn't have any paper which showed me from which group they came or from which people.
Once there came a man who was very obviously Jewish.
There was no doubt about for me that he was a Jewish man.
And-- but he had no paper and nothing.
And he came to our house, and he said, I am so-and-so.
That's my name.
And I have heard that you help people and I'm going to be caught tonight.
I know that.
Because they are coming to my house and I have nowhere to go.
And I know that you hide people.
Will you please hide me?
I said, well, I-- why-- why should I hide you?
I don't know you.
And he said, well, I know that you have to play innocent.
But I know that you are hiding Jewish people.
I said, well, I don't know what you know.
But that's certainly not the truth.
And, well, I talked for about half an hour for him before I finally got rid of him.
And two or three nights later, I was-- my husband and I had to go out a moment, you know.
Sometimes it was crazy with all those people.
And they-- all the Jewish people didn't even like each other.
And women would fight, and all-- it was a difficult situation.

So one night I said to my husband, let's get out and have a cup of coffee-- which they called coffee, there was no real coffee anymore-- and so we went to this bar, of-- or, restaurant, and or that coffee-- some brown stuff that they called coffee-- and I saw, in another table, I saw there that man who had tried to hide with me, with a group of Jewish-- of German soldiers-- officers.
First of all, I ran away from there, when I was-- I didn't want to see him and I didn't want him to see me.
But then I knew that I had done the right thing, that he was a traitor.
And that was a rather narrow escape because I almost fell for it.
But he had none of the papers that were needed.
Or none of the names that were needed.
And so with a breaking heart, I let him go.
I suffered.
It was-- it was maybe a week later that I then saw him with those Germans.
But that whole week, I felt, it's so terrible that I have refused him.
Maybe he's shot.
Maybe he's dead.
Maybe I could have helped him.
You know.
But then later, I knew I had done the right thing.
And I cried.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS]
